source.title: The role of dorsal raphe nucleus serotonergic and non-serotonergic neurons, and of their receptors, in regulating waking and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep

confidence_rationale: Canonical review establishing the DRN serotonin waking-high / REM-silent firing pattern. Directly supports the claim that tonic 5-HT drops during sleep phases, enabling the replay-gating mechanism MECH-203 proposes. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed review, authoritative author). Mapping fidelity is moderate: the review documents state-dependent firing rates rather than replay prioritization per se, so the specific benefit-salience tagging function requires additional inferential steps.
failure_signatures: 5-HT receptor subtypes (1A vs 2A/C) have opposing effects -- if MECH-203 is receptor-subtype-specific, this heterogeneity introduces ambiguity
non-serotonergic DRN neurons (dopamine, GABA, glutamate) contribute to sleep regulation independently -- tonic 5-HT is not the sole DRN output

mapping.source_claim_statement: Serotonin promotes waking and inhibits REM sleep; DRN serotonergic neurons fire maximally during wake, decrease during NREM, and become virtually silent during REM. Receptor knockouts (5-HT1A, 5-HT1B) increase REM sleep; 5-HT2A/C knockouts increase wakefulness.
mapping.ree_translation: The waking-high / REM-silent 5-HT discharge pattern establishes the tonic level gradient that MECH-203 requires: during waking, high tonic 5-HT tags benefit-relevant experiences; during REM, near-zero 5-HT releases these tags for replay processing. The phase boundary (waking -> NREM -> REM) is implemented by DRN activity descent, matching the SWS-then-REM ordering specified in MECH-203's notes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review addresses 5-HT's role in sleep-stage gating, not replay prioritization directly. The benefit-salience tagging function is an additional theoretical layer not directly tested in this literature. Transfer risk from cat/rat electrophysiology to human REE substrate is moderate.
mapping.source_context: Comprehensive review of DRN neurochemistry across sleep states. Covers receptor subtype pharmacology (1A, 1B, 2A/C, 3, 7), microinjection studies, and knockout phenotypes. Establishes mechanistic precision about which receptor subtypes govern which sleep transitions.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high: authoritative review in top sleep journal, comprehensive receptor pharmacology. Mapping fidelity moderate: source confirms the tonic gradient exists but does not address replay salience tagging. Transfer risk moderate: animal electrophysiology (cat/rat) to computational model.
